meta.title: The Daily Reflection Tree
meta.description: A deterministic end-of-day reflection tool walking through three psychological axes: Locus (Victim↔Victor), Orientation (Entitlement↔Contribution), and Radius (Self-Centric↔Altrocentric).

nodes.text: Good evening. Before you close today, let's take ten minutes to look at it honestly. Not to judge — just to see. Ready when you are.
Let's start simple. If today were a weather report, what would it be?
options: Sunny — things went well and I felt in control
Cloudy — mixed, some friction but I got through it
Stormy — rough, I felt pushed around by events
Foggy — unclear, I wasn't sure what I was doing or why
answer=Sunny — things went well and I felt in control|Cloudy — mixed, some friction but I got through it:A1_Q_AGENCY_HIGH
answer=Stormy — rough, I felt pushed around by events|Foggy — unclear, I wasn't sure what I was doing or why:A1_Q_AGENCY_LOW

nodes.text: You described today as "{A1_OPEN.answer}". When something went well, what do you think made it happen?
options: I prepared or anticipated it
I adjusted quickly when things shifted
The team or someone else made it work
It just happened to go right — not sure why

nodes.text: You described today as "{A1_OPEN.answer}". When something went wrong, what was your first instinct?
options: Figure out what I could still control
Wait for someone else to fix it or step in
Push through alone, even if frustrated
Feel stuck — like the situation had me boxed in
answer=I prepared or anticipated it|I adjusted quickly when things shifted:A1_Q_CHOICE_STRONG
answer=The team or someone else made it work|It just happened to go right — not sure why:A1_Q_CHOICE_WEAK
answer=Figure out what I could still control:A1_Q_CHOICE_STRONG
answer=Wait for someone else to fix it or step in|Push through alone, even if frustrated|Feel stuck — like the situation had me boxed in:A1_Q_CHOICE_WEAK

nodes.text: Think of one moment today where you made a deliberate choice — even a small one. What kind of choice was it?
options: How I communicated something difficult
Deciding to ask for help rather than go it alone
Choosing to keep going when I wanted to stop
Setting a boundary or saying no to something

nodes.text: Think of one moment today where things felt out of your hands. Looking back now — was there a small choice you didn't see at the time?
options: Yes — I could have spoken up earlier
Yes — I could have asked for what I needed
Maybe — it's still not totally clear to me
No — it genuinely wasn't in my control
state=axis1.internal>=2:A1_R_INTERNAL
state=axis1.internal<2:A1_R_EXTERNAL

nodes.text: You stayed in the driver's seat today — even when the road was rough. That's not a small thing. Agency isn't about controlling what happens. It's about knowing you're not just a passenger. You lived that today.

Today pulled your attention outward — to what others did, to what didn't go right. That's understandable; sometimes the current is strong. But somewhere in there, you made at least one call. Even if it was small. It counts.
You've looked at how you navigated today. Now let's look at what you gave — and what you expected.
You've been honest about today's friction. Now let's look at a different angle — what you gave, and what you felt was owed.
Think about your interactions today. Which of these best describes a moment that stood out?
options: I helped someone with something that wasn't technically my job
I felt like my effort wasn't being seen or credited
I did something extra — proactively, without being asked
I felt frustrated that others weren't carrying their weight
answer=I helped someone with something that wasn't technically my job|I did something extra — proactively, without being asked:A2_Q_CONTRIBUTION
answer=I felt like my effort wasn't being seen or credited|I felt frustrated that others weren't carrying their weight:A2_Q_ENTITLEMENT

nodes.text: When you gave that extra effort — what was driving it?
options: I genuinely wanted to help the person or the outcome
I hoped it would be noticed and acknowledged
It was just the right thing to do in the moment
I'd want someone to do the same for me

nodes.text: When you felt that frustration — what were you expecting that didn't come?
options: Recognition for work I know was good
Others to match my level of effort
Fairness — being treated the way I treat others
To be consulted or included more
answer=I genuinely wanted to help the person or the outcome|It was just the right thing to do in the moment:A2_Q_DISCRETIONARY
answer=I hoped it would be noticed and acknowledged|I'd want someone to do the same for me:A2_Q_RECIPROCAL
answer=Recognition for work I know was good|To be consulted or included more:A2_Q_RECIPROCAL
answer=Others to match my level of effort|Fairness — being treated the way I treat others:A2_Q_DISCRETIONARY

nodes.text: Is there something you chose NOT to give today — effort, attention, or help — that you could have?
options: No — I was genuinely at capacity
Yes — I held back because I was frustrated
Yes — I didn't think it was my place
Maybe — I didn't notice the opportunity until now

nodes.text: When you think about what you feel owed — recognition, effort, inclusion — what would change if you let that go, even temporarily?
options: I'd probably feel lighter and less frustrated
Nothing — the expectation still seems fair
I might focus more on what I actually did
I'm not sure — it's hard to imagine
state=axis2.contribution>=2:A2_R_CONTRIBUTION
state=axis2.contribution<2:A2_R_ENTITLEMENT

nodes.text: Today you gave more than was asked. That discretionary effort — the help that wasn't in your job description, the work nobody tracked — that's what organizational trust is actually built from. It doesn't always show up in the metrics. But it's real.

Wanting recognition for real work isn't wrong. But notice where your attention went today — toward what you received, or toward what you created. The two lead to very different kinds of days. You get to choose which one you track tomorrow.
You've looked at how you showed up and what you gave. One more lens — let's zoom out and see who else was in your world today.
You've been honest about what you were tracking today. Now let's try widening the frame — who else was in the picture?
When you think about today's biggest challenge or moment — who comes to mind first?
options: Mostly me — it was my problem, my stress, my deadline
My immediate team — we were all navigating it together
A specific colleague who had it harder than I did
The customer, end user, or person this work ultimately serves
answer=Mostly me — it was my problem, my stress, my deadline:A3_Q_SELF
answer=My immediate team — we were all navigating it together:A3_Q_TEAM
answer=A specific colleague who had it harder than I did|The customer, end user, or person this work ultimately serves:A3_Q_WIDE

nodes.text: That focus on yourself isn't wrong — sometimes the work really is personal. But: was there anyone else affected by how your day went, even indirectly?
options: Yes — my stress probably affected how I showed up for others
Yes — my output affects teammates downstream
Maybe — I hadn't thought about it that way
Not really — this was genuinely self-contained

nodes.text: You were thinking about the team. Was there a specific person whose experience you found yourself actually considering — not just as a coworker, but as someone going through something?
options: Yes — someone specific came to mind, and I checked in
Yes — I noticed, but didn't do anything
Not really — I was more focused on the group outcome
I hadn't noticed anyone in particular until now

nodes.text: You were thinking beyond yourself — about a colleague or the person your work serves. What did that awareness change, if anything, about how you acted today?
options: It made me more patient or careful with my work
It made me more willing to help even when I was tired
It kept me going when I felt like stopping
I noticed it but it didn't change much
answer=Yes — my stress probably affected how I showed up for others|Yes — my output affects teammates downstream:A3_Q_EXPAND
answer=Maybe — I hadn't thought about it that way|Not really — this was genuinely self-contained:A3_Q_MEANING
answer=Yes — someone specific came to mind, and I checked in|Yes — I noticed, but didn't do anything:A3_Q_EXPAND
answer=Not really — I was more focused on the group outcome|I hadn't noticed anyone in particular until now:A3_Q_MEANING
answer=It made me more patient or careful with my work|It made me more willing to help even when I was tired|It kept me going when I felt like stopping:A3_Q_EXPAND
answer=I noticed it but it didn't change much:A3_Q_MEANING

nodes.text: When you think about the person or people beyond yourself who your work touched today — what's one thing you'd want them to be able to say about how you showed up?
options: That I was reliable — they could count on me
That I was present — I actually paid attention to them
That I made something harder a little easier for them
That I didn't make their day worse

nodes.text: Sometimes we lose sight of who the work is for. If you could zoom out and see one person whose day was better because you showed up today — who would it realistically be?
options: A teammate who needed my output to do their job
A customer or user who benefited from what I produced
My manager or team — by me not being a problem
I'm genuinely not sure — today felt disconnected from impact
state=axis3.altrocentric>=2:A3_R_ALTROCENTRIC
state=axis3.altrocentric<2:A3_R_SELF

nodes.text: You moved beyond the frame of your own experience today. That's not easy, especially on hard days. Maslow called it self-transcendence — the shift from 'what do I need?' to 'what does this moment need from me?' You touched that today.

Today's frame stayed close — your stress, your goals, your experience. That's human. But here's something worth sitting with: most of the meaning we find in work lives at the edge of our concern for others. It's worth noticing who else is in the picture tomorrow.
That's it. You looked at your day honestly. That's more than most people do. See you tomorrow.

axis1.internal: You saw your hand in what happened — the choices you made, the adaptations you navigated.
axis1.external: Today felt like something that happened to you, more than something you moved through.

axis2.contribution: You were thinking about what you could give, not just what you deserved.
axis2.entitlement: Your attention went toward what you were owed — recognition, effort, fairness.

axis3.altrocentric: Your frame included others — their experience, their needs, their stake in the day.
axis3.self: The frame stayed close today — which makes sense, but leaves something on the table.

closing_questions.internal_contribution_altrocentric: What would it look like to bring this same energy deliberately tomorrow — not by accident?
closing_questions.internal_contribution_self: You acted well and gave generously. Who else was in the room today that you didn't quite see?
closing_questions.internal_entitlement_altrocentric: You stayed agentive and you thought about others. Where did the scorekeeping come in — and what would happen if you dropped it?
closing_questions.internal_entitlement_self: You navigated well, but you were keeping score. What would you focus on instead if the score didn't matter?
closing_questions.external_contribution_altrocentric: You gave more than you realized, and you thought about others. Where did you give away your sense of agency?
closing_questions.external_contribution_self: You gave today. What would it take to also feel like you were steering?
closing_questions.external_entitlement_self: You felt pushed around and underrewarded. Those might both be true. But where was the one moment you had more control than you used?
closing_questions.external_entitlement_altrocentric: You saw others clearly even when today felt hard. Can you turn that same generosity of perception toward your own choices tomorrow?
